title: Cathead biscuits
subtitle: American Southern drop biscuits, large and rough
excerpt: Cathead biscuits are large American Southern drop biscuits, shaped by dropping rough mounds of soft dough from a spoon rather than rolling and cutting. The name refers to their size: each biscuit is as big as a cat's head. The dough is wetter than a rolled biscuit, which makes them faster to make and gives a softer, more irregular crumb.
sourceNotes: Cathead biscuits are a cornerstone of Southern Appalachian cooking and appear in American regional foodways documentation from the nineteenth century onward. They are associated with the biscuit culture of the American South where large, hand-formed biscuits were a staple of breakfast and supper. The drop method, where wet dough is spooned straight onto the tray, appears in domestic records as a faster alternative to the rolled biscuit. The name is consistent across Southern regional cookery sources.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked biscuits. Reheat from frozen at 170 degrees C fan for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten fresh. Reheat split in a dry pan if making ahead.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 15 to 20 minutes until the peaks are deep gold and the base is set

content.text: Cathead biscuits are made with a wet, scoopable dough that would be impossible to roll. This is the point: the rough, domed surface bakes to a crust while the inside stays soft and slightly pillowy. They are traditionally baked touching each other in a cast-iron skillet, which gives crisp sides where they meet. A baking tray works equally well. Do not smooth or flatten them before baking: the rough surface is how they get their crust.

Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Grease a baking tray or a 26 cm cast-iron skillet. Combine 300 g plain flour, 2.5 tsp baking powder, and three-quarters of a teaspoon of salt in a large bowl. Add 75 g cold diced butter and rub in until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Pour in 250 ml whole milk and 75 g soured cream if using. Stir with a fork until a shaggy, wet dough forms: it should be too soft to knead. Using a large spoon or ice cream scoop, drop 8 rough mounds onto the tray, leaving a little space between each. They should be about 8 cm across and look rough and uneven.

Bake for 15 to 20 minutes until the rough peaks are deep gold and the base is set and pulling from the tin. The interior should be cooked through and not doughy when you break one open. Brush with melted butter immediately after baking if you want a richer crust. Leave for 5 minutes before serving, split.

items.problem: Biscuits are dense with a thick, gummy interior
items.solution: The dough was overworked, or the biscuits were too large. Stir only enough to bring the dough together, and keep each biscuit to no more than 8 cm across and 5 cm high.

items.problem: The surface is pale and the outside is soft
items.solution: The oven was not hot enough. The high temperature is essential for the crust. Check the oven temperature with a thermometer and make sure it is fully preheated before the biscuits go in.
